unitTitle: GCSE Algorithms part 2 — searching and sorting (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Linear search
lessons.objectives:
I can say why computers need to search data
I can carry out a linear search to find an item in a list
I can count the comparisons a linear search makes
I can give the best-case and worst-case for a linear search
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: linear search · list · comparison · ordered · unordered · best case · worst case. TA: hand out the playing cards (or printed cards) per group. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — searching for a book (think–pair–share). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter worksheet whether an ordered or unordered shelf is quicker. Draw out ordered vs unordered.
   • Support: tick what a linear search does.  • Core: write one real search.  • Challenge: why an unordered list forces a linear search.
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do. Cups demo: a computer cannot see the whole list, it checks one item at a time from the start. Pupils drag the linear-search STEPS into order on the activity worksheet.
   • Support: word-bank/order task.  • Core: count comparisons. Likely error: forgetting the “or reach the end” stop → fix-words “a linear search also stops when the list runs out.”
3. (15–35) You-do — unplugged: in groups, place cards face down and turn over ONE at a time to find a chosen card; fill the table; one run searches for a removed card (not found).
   • Support: arrow card “check this one next”.  • Core: record comparisons.  • Challenge: convert the steps to Python (Parsons on the worksheet). TA: prompt, do not turn the cards for them.
4. (35–50) Best and worst case — best = item is first (1 comparison); worst = item is last or not in the list (check them all). Worked example with the cities list.
5. (50–55) Plenary — pupils self-tick the four “I can…”; ask the worst-case number of comparisons for 7 items (answer 7).
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: searching hook (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Binary search
lessons.objectives:
I can carry out a binary search on ordered data
I can say why the list must be in order
I can find the position of an item with a binary search
I can say when a binary search cannot be used
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: binary search · ordered · midpoint · middle-left · divide and conquer. TA: cards in order per group. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — find the number (8 ordered boxes, 3 looks). Pupils CHOOSE to look in the middle box first. Draw out that the middle rules out half the boxes.
   • Support: tick that binary search needs ordered data.  • Core: why the middle rules out the most.  • Challenge: which search if the boxes were jumbled.
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do. Cups demo of divide and conquer: check the midpoint, throw away the half it cannot be in, repeat. Midpoint with an even number = MIDDLE-LEFT item. Pupils drag the binary-search STEPS into order and SORT facts (linear vs binary) on the worksheet.
   • Support: sort-the-facts task.  • Core: before/after the midpoint. Likely error: not discarding a half → fix-words “after every check, half the list is gone.”
3. (15–35) You-do — unplugged: cards in order, face down; turn over the MIDDLE card first; fill the table; one run for a not-found card.
   • Support: “turn the middle first” prompt.  • Core: one full search.  • Challenge: doubling the list adds only ~1 comparison. TA: prompt, do not turn cards for them.
4. (35–50) When can’t you use it? Binary search needs ordered data; otherwise use linear or sort first — a hook for the sorting lessons.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; name one thing binary search needs that linear search does not (the data in order).
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Comparing searching algorithms
lessons.objectives:
I can compare linear and binary search and pick the best one
I can read the Python code for a linear search
I can trace a search with a set of data
I can explain what returning -1 means
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: linear search · binary search · trace table · Python · efficiency. TA: pupils have the worksheet + a paper trace table. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — finding a word in a dictionary (ordered). Pupils CHOOSE that both searches work because it is in order. Recap worst case.
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do. Which search, and when? Linear works on ANY data but is slower; binary needs ORDERED data but is faster on large lists. Pupils drag scenarios into a SORT (use linear / use binary).
   • Support: tick which search works on unordered data.  • Core: comparisons to find Neptune by linear search (5).  • Challenge: a reason to still choose linear search.
3. (15–35) Reading the code (I do). Embed the linear-search Python code (Figure 1); build it up from the inside out (one comparison, then the loop). Pupils answer “which line is the loop” (line 4) and the function/loop questions. Run the linear-search demo for the steps.
   • Support: which line repeats.  • Challenge: order the FASTER version (with a found flag) as a Parsons. Likely error: thinking it must reach the end → fix-words “found = True lets it stop early.”
4. (35–50) Trace it (you do). Trace the search for Neptune on a trace table, row by row, and screenshot it. Explain that -1 means “not found”.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; one reason to choose linear over binary (unordered / short / changing data).
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: linear search code (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Bubble sort
lessons.objectives:
I can say why computers need to sort data
I can do one pass of a bubble sort, swapping items that are out of order
I can carry out a full bubble sort
I can label the parts of one bubble-sort pass
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: bubble sort · pass · compare · swap. Avoid “bubbling up” (the largest item sinks to the end). TA: cards per pupil/pair. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — finding a surname in a jumbled vs ordered copy. Pupils CHOOSE the ordered copy. Big theme: we sort to make searching faster.
   • Support: tick what sorting is for.  • Core: a real sorted dataset.  • Challenge: how sorting first helps binary search.
2. (5–18) I-do → we-do. Compare two NEIGHBOURS, swap if out of order, move along — one pass. Step through the card picture (embedded); after one pass the largest item is at the end; comparisons per pass = items − 1. Pupils order the one-pass STEPS.
   • Support: order-the-steps.  • Core: which item is sorted after one pass / comparisons in a 7-item pass (6). Likely error: comparing items far apart → fix-words “only ever the two next to each other.”
3. (18–35) You-do — LABEL the embedded bubble-pass picture (a swap, a no-swap, the biggest card at the end), then carry out a FULL bubble sort of the cuisines list and screenshot it.
   • Support: labels + arrow prompts.  • Core: full sort.  • Challenge: why one fewer pair each pass. TA: prompt, do not sort it for them.
4. (35–50) Review — keep doing passes until no swaps are needed; the next-largest item lands in place each pass.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; which item is sorted after the first pass (the biggest).
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: one pass of a bubble sort (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Insertion sort
lessons.objectives:
I can insert an item into the right place in an ordered list
I can describe how an insertion sort works
I can name the sorted and unsorted parts of the list
I can carry out a full insertion sort
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: insertion sort · sorted sublist · unsorted sublist · insert. (Check the exam board — some do not require insertion sort.) TA: cards per pupil/pair. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — sorting a hand of cards / a new book on an ordered shelf (embedded hand-of-cards picture). Pupils CHOOSE “slide the others along and slot it in”.
   • Support: tick that each card goes into its correct place.  • Core: which algorithm this is like.  • Challenge: how “slot it in” differs from bubble sort’s swap.
2. (5–18) I-do — inserting ONE item (embedded insert-a-card picture). The sorted part grows at the start; bigger sorted items slide one place RIGHT to make a gap; the held value goes in the gap (stress the variable). Pupils order the insert STEPS and SORT insertion-vs-bubble facts.
   • Support: order-the-steps + sort task.  • Core: which part the sorted sublist starts as (just the first item). Likely error: doing a bubble sort instead → fix-words “find the gap and slide, do not just swap neighbours.”
3. (18–38) You-do — carry out a FULL insertion sort of the cuisines list, shading the sorted part each pass; screenshot it.
   • Support: partially-worked first pass.  • Core: full sort.  • Challenge: the two pass-end conditions. TA: prompt, do not sort it for them.
4. (38–52) Review — recap the sorted/unsorted parts and how each pass moves one item across.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; name the two parts the list is split into.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: a hand of cards (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

resources.title: inserting a card (Teach Computing).png

lessons.title: Coding sorting algorithms
lessons.objectives:
I can read and explain the code for a bubble sort
I can put the swap lines in the right order
I can trace the swap part of a bubble sort
I can identify a change that makes a bubble sort more efficient
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: bubble sort · insertion sort · trace table · pass · swap · temp variable. TA: pupils have the worksheet + a paper trace table. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — true or false about bubble and insertion sort. Pupils TICK ALL the TRUE statements (multi-select; bubble sort is NOT the fastest for large lists).
   • Support: tick that both put data into order.  • Core: one difference between them.  • Challenge: why bubble sort can be fine for small, nearly-sorted lists.
2. (5–20) I-do. Embed the bubble-sort Python code; build it up from the inside out (outer loop = passes; inner loop = compares each pair and swaps). Run the bubble-sort demo for the steps.
   • Support: the outer loop counts the passes.  • Core: order the three SWAP lines (Parsons) and explain the temp variable. Likely error: overwriting without temp → fix-words “save the first value before you copy over it.”
3. (20–38) You-do — trace lines 7–9 (one swap) on a trace table; screenshot it. Two improvements: check one fewer pair each pass (num_items − passes) and stop early if no swaps.
   • Support: recap the code labels.  • Core: the swap trace.  • Challenge: why “no swaps” means already sorted. TA: prompt, do not trace it for them.
4. (38–52) Review — insertion sort code is similar but copies items along instead of swapping pairs.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; why a swap needs a temp variable.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: bubble sort code (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Merge sort
lessons.objectives:
I can merge two ordered lists into one ordered list
I can describe the split stage and the merge stage
I can carry out a full merge sort
I can say what merge sort divides
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: merge · split · sort · divide and conquer. TA: cards per pupil/pair. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — combining two ordered groups of birthdays into one ordered group. Pupils CHOOSE “keep taking the earlier of the two front birthdays”.
   • Support: tick that merging two ordered groups gives an ordered group.  • Core: why ordered groups are easy to merge.  • Challenge: why looking only at the FRONT of each list is enough.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do. Merging two ordered lists (embedded merge picture): compare the FRONT of each, move the smaller, repeat. Pupils order the MERGE steps; then the whole-algorithm SPLIT-then-MERGE steps.
   • Support: merge-steps order task.  • Core: split-then-merge order task; what merge sort divides (the list). Likely error: comparing the wrong items → fix-words “only the front of each list.”
3. (20–38) You-do — carry out a FULL merge sort of the dog-breeds list: split to single items, then merge pairs back together; screenshot it.
   • Support: empty split/merge tables as scaffolds.  • Core: full sort.  • Challenge: why merge sort always merges in PAIRS (odd item joins the first list; odd list waits). TA: prompt; draw clear boxes around each list.
4. (38–52) More about merge sort — fast on large lists (divide and conquer); recursion not needed for GCSE.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; the two stages of a merge sort (split, then merge).
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: merging two ordered lists (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Algorithms review
lessons.objectives:
I can choose the right searching or sorting algorithm for a list
I can carry out a sort and then a search on a list
I can describe how each algorithm works
I can compare two algorithms and justify a choice
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: linear search · binary search · bubble sort · insertion sort · merge sort · trace table. TA: pupils have the worksheet + paper. Read the four “I can…” aloud. This lesson revises for the assessment.
1. (0–5) Starter — mystery code (a linear search with hidden variable names). Pupils CHOOSE that it is a linear search; reveal one question at a time.
   • Support: the while line repeats.  • Core: a clearer variable name.  • Challenge: how to let it stop early.
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do. SORT the five algorithms into searches vs sorts. Recap: searches find an item; sorts put items in order so searching is faster.
3. (15–35) You-do — the song list is unordered, so only a linear search works until we sort it. Pupils answer the search questions, order a bubble-sort pass, then SORT bubble-vs-merge facts, then bubble sort the songs and binary search “Shallow”; screenshot.
   • Support: sort-the-algorithms.  • Core: which sort does what + the bubble pass order.  • Challenge: which sort for a large list, with a reason. Likely error: trying binary search on unsorted data → fix-words “sort first, then binary search.” TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (35–50) Compare bubble sort and merge sort — bubble is simple but slow; merge is faster (divide and conquer). Thumbs up/down whole-class check.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; one algorithm and one fact about it. Homework: revise for the assessment.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Summative assessment
lessons.objectives:
I can develop a linear search function in Python
I can choose the best search or sort for a situation
I can trace a sorting algorithm
I can use the correct computational thinking terms
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: computational thinking · linear search · binary search · bubble sort · insertion sort · merge sort · trace table. Keep the room calm and predictable — this is the assessment lesson. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–15) Starter — build a linear search function. Pupils order the lines of the function (Parsons) on the starter worksheet; the comments are the guide. Show the starter code in repl.it; walk through the success criteria, especially the -1 return.
   • Support: what the function returns (the index).  • Core: what it returns when not found (-1).  • Challenge: where to set a found flag.
2. (15–45) Assessment — pupils complete the end-of-unit quiz worksheet independently, in silence, like an exam. The quiz folds the unit’s summative questions into supported types: computational-thinking terms, which-algorithm, bubble-sort passes, a true-statements multi-select, a swap trace (screenshot), and an improvement. Allow extra time and movement breaks as normal.
   • Support: recognition (terms, which algorithm).  • Core: bubble-sort passes / which search.  • Challenge: the true-statements set, the trace, and the efficiency improvement.
3. (45–52) Recap — computational thinking (decomposition, abstraction, algorithmic thinking) and a one-line recap of each searching/sorting algorithm. End on the big idea: sorting data makes searching it faster.
4. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; confidence rating to spot anything to revisit.
Differentiation on the SAME assessment, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
